repressed memories and to gain conviction about these repressed memories. In this treatment-transference model, transference interpretations focusing on the analyst are not germane, since the transferences are merely the vehicle by which memories are uncovered. Thus, Freud's transference work in 1907 revealed a transitional phase of his clinical activity soon to be supplanted by later insights into the curative aspects of transference analysis.

Harold Blum  

MY FOCUS hERE is upon the vital importance of insight for psychoanalytic therapy, theory, and technique. In affirming the curative and creative roles of insight, I am not subscribing to its idealization or assigning to it magical qualities. In analysis, insight is the one element that is never in excess and yet is never complete. I shall maintain that insight propels the psychoanalytic process forward and is a condition, catalyst, and consequence of the psychoanalytic process. There is a circular interaction between the development of insight and productive analytic work leading to structural change.

Obtaining insights, of course, is not confined to psychoanalytic situations. Artists and poets, more in contact with the unconscious, probed and provided insights into the human mind that preceded and probably facilitated psychoanalytic discoveries. If such insights did not necessarily have therapeutic effects, creative persons such as Sophocles and Shakespeare achieved extraordinary understanding of normal fantasy, psychic conflict, and character disorder that continue to inspire psychoanalytic study.

Despite the punitive expulsion from paradise for gaining forbidden knowledge, the quest for insight has always paralleled the "bliss of ignorance." The hero of the oedipal myth moves and wavers between insight and blindness. Oedipus seeks the truth while asserting innocence of his guilt and ignorance of his identity. Analogous to the analytic process, the oedipal myth depicts the struggle between insight and resistance to insight.

SUMMARY

Freud used the term idealization in reference to a variety of concepts. Subsequent authors, not always specifying their meanings, have tended to apply the term to only one or only some of these concepts. This paper surveys Freud's explicit and implicit references to idealization in the context of compatible and integrated psychoanalytic theories of drives and of object relations.

… But I am the opposite of a stage magician. He gives you illusion that has the appearance of truth. I give you truth in the pleasant disguise of illusion.

-Tennessee Williams, The Glass Menagerie

FROM ITS BEGINNING, PSYCHOANALYSIS has thought of itself as a genetic psychology, regarding each behavior as part of a historical sequence determined by epigenetic laws and cumulative experience (Freud, 1905). To put a finer epistemological edge on the preceding, each behavior has been thought of as part of a genetic series and, through its antecedents, part of the temporal sequence that brought about the present form of the personality and its psychic organization (Rapaport and Gill, 1959). In this sense, the infantile, the adult, and the transference neuroses may be considered to be joined referents within a genetic series. Even more pertinent to the greater methodological question before this panel1 is the adductive relationship assumed to exist between the infantile and transference neuroses.

The Restoration of the Self

By Heinz Kohut. New York: International Universities Press, Inc., 1977, xxii+345 pp., $17.50.

Martin Stein

Martin Stein

Martin H. Stein
New York City

Martin Stein  

Interest in the self is very much with us. We are to live life to the fullest as individuals, a doctrine popularly expressed as "doing our thing," which includes not only loving oneself, being creative and joyful; it often encompasses activities that are disconcerting, at least to other, nonself, entities. Thought, delayed gratification, attempts to resolve inner conflict in reasonable ways tend rather to be de-emphasized as part of the relegation of secondary process to a subordinate position in the hierarchy of desirable developments in human psychic life. This trend has been associated with adverse criticisms of the methods and principles of modern ("Western") science, with its emphasis on neutrality and objectivity, and its rejection of much historical study.

Concern with the self has not been entirely neglected in psychoanalytic writings: Edith Jacobson's The Self and the Object World is a significant attempt to deal with problems of narcissism in the context of object relations theory; Otto Kernberg has published a series of papers dealing with the same theme, with even more emphasis on the importance of extremely early object relations. In the works of Kohut, however, we find the "self" dealt with as an entity separate and central in human development, its formation and growth perhaps even more important than later or parallel evolvement of relations to objects; and we are presented with a new term: self-object. His first volume, The Analysis of the Self (1971)

[[RunningHead]]

Some Reflections on Boredom

Aaron Esman M.D.

Aaron Esman

Aaron H. Esman, M.D.
120 West 57 Street
New York, New York 10019

Aaron Esman  

SUMMARY

Boredom is a complex mental phenomenon, incorporating both affective and cognitive components, which cannot be understood within the confines of conflict theory alone. The paucity of fantasy in the bored person may reflect a developmental ego defect as well as, or instead of, the commonlycited impulse-defense conflicts. The need for externally-supplied stimulation characteristic of persons with a field-dependent cognitive style, and/or characterological passivity may be among the significant predisposing factors for the development of boredom, and phase-specific factors may encourage its occurrence at particular periods of life. Boredom in the analyst, too, is multiply determined; the analytic situation itself contains features that contribute to it.

John McDevitt M.D.

John B. McDevitt, M.D.

John McDevitt  

MANY AUTHORS—Jacobson (1964), Mahler and Furer (1968), Mahler et al. (1975), Spitz (1957), (1965)—as well as the members of today's panel (Gedo and Goldberg, 1973); (Kanzer, 1957); (Lichtenberg, 1975); (Meissner, 1971); (Spruiell, 1974) have pointed out that incorporation, imitation and identification, and introjection play a significant role in the development of object relations.

In this paper I intend first to examine a few aspects of the subphase-specific development of internalization in terms of its role in beginning the establishment of object relations.

Presented in a shortened version at the Panel on Current Concepts of Object Relations Theory, Fall Meeting of The American Psychoanalytic Association, December 1977.

[[RunningHead]]

Freud's Project Reassessed

By Karl H. Pribram and Merton M. Gill. New York: Basic Books, 1976, 192 pp., $10.00.

Herbert Weiner M.D.

Herbert Weiner

Herbert Weiner, M.D.
Montefiore Hospital and Medical Center

Herbert Weiner  

Freud's unpublished "Project for a Scientific Psychology" is intimately related to his greatest, published, theoretical works, such as "The Interpretation of Dreams" and his metapsychological papers. This

215

is how Leonardo da Vinci's Notebooks are related to his paintings and other creations. Neither the "Project" nor the Notebooks were supposed to see the light of day—in fact, Leonardo wrote his Notebooks in mirror writing with his left hand. The "Project" seems to have been Freud's notebook in which he sketched out most of his ensuing concepts and hypotheses, largely in neuronal terms. Later, he disclaimed on several occasions that Mind could be described in neuronal or neural terms. Perhaps, with his fine taste, that is why he never published the "Project."

As the authors of this reassessment of the "Project" point out, the "Project" has not been taken seriously in academic circles since first published and translated. Yet the problems it addresses are central to the concerns of anyone interested in the mind. In the United States particularly, concerns about the mind have only recently reasserted themselves in academic circles—concerns with judgment, thought, attention, perception, memory, motivation, and intention, rather than only with learning, conditioning, and behavior.

The "Project" is a rich source book for psychologists. It is tailor-made for the historian of ideas, of psychology, and of psychoanalysis. Yet it is a truly curious document: rich in its connotations, an essential guide to most mental phenomena, laden with passages that are almost beyond comprehension (as many works of genius are), and crucial to an understanding of all the problems that metapsychology poses. To put it bluntly—it is an inspired and perplexing work of genius.

Freud begins slowly in the "Project," building on suppositions that seem to come from nowhere. Could he have known about Ramon y Cajal's lecture in 1889, or Waldeyer's "neuron doctrine," both of which proclaim that the brain is not a continuous syncytial network, but rather is composed of discontinuous elements—neurons—that are connected with each other? He proclaims that neurons exist, that junctions connect them, and that such junctions create barriers to impulse flow. But he does so two years before Foster and Sherrington, in 1897, named these junctions synapses!

[[RunningHead]]

Zur Theorie, Technik Und Therapie Der Psychoanalyse

By Wolfgang Loch. Edited by Thure von Uexküll and Ilse Grubrich-Simitis. Frankfurt am Main: S. Fischer Verlag, 1972, 428 pp.

Ernst Ticho

Ernst Ticho

Ernst A. Ticho
Washington, D.C.

Ernst Ticho  

This book is one of a series of publications dealing with the conditio humana, a collection that contains, among other works, important psychoanalytic contributions that have long been unavailable in German—a study edition of Freud's works in ten volumes, with commentaries, the first such edition in German, as well as the work of Anna Freud, Dorothy Burlingham, Karl Abraham, and Sándor Ferenczi. It might be of interest to the non-German reader that S. Fischer Verlag was the most outstanding publishing house in Germany before and after the First World War (they published Thomas Mann, among others) and after having been suppressed by the Nazis have today regained their preeminent position.

Dr. Wolfgang Loch is Professor of Psychoanalysis on the Medical Faculty at the University of Tübingen. He is also an internist and a neurologist. This collection of his papers, originally published between 1959 and 1970, reflects the wide scope of Loch's interests: it contains articles on theory, technique, and therapy, as well as a section devoted to psychoanalytic contributions to the understanding of psychosis.

In their introduction, the editors call attention to the main thrust of Loch's book—the danger that authoritarian education imposes for the development of the superego. The editors further stress that inasmuch as the author deals with basic problems of the conditio humana—with anxiety, aggression, suicide, psychosis, myth, choice of partners—his work deserves to be placed in this series.

Loch sets himself a very difficult task and succeeds in its execution. In nearly all of his papers he deals not only with the analytic history of his subject, beginning with Freud, but also with the current state of psychoanalytic thinking; he tries to bridge this latter with German psychiatry, philosophy, and anthropology. He has a highly cultivated audience in mind, but is aware that his readers may not be as well-grounded in psychoanalysis as he would perhaps like. He has to be didactic, knowing full well that the years of Nazism tore a deep gap in the knowledge of his older readers. But he has not only to teach analysis, he must also attack widespread prejudices against it, and this he has done most effectively.

Libido Und Gesellschaft: Studien Über Freud Und Die Freudsche Linke [libido and Society. Studies about Freud and the Freudian Left]

By Helmut Dahmer. Frankfurt am Main: Alexander Mitscherlich Suhrkamp Verlag, 1973, 465 pp.

Peter Novotny M.D.

Peter Novotny

Peter C. Novotny, M.D.
The Menninger Foundation

Peter Novotny  

264

Libido and Society sets out to show how psychoanalysis reflected the historical period of its creation. The psychoanalytic view of human nature, according to Helmut Dahmer, reflects what bourgeois society was like at the end of the nineteenth century. Freud's theoretical conceptualizations, to the extent to which they were modeled on the natural-science tradition, were a product of economic conditions. Psychoanalysis as well as idealistic German philosophy can thus be understood as mere superstructure for the prominent substructure, i.e., the means of production. What Freud observed, how he conceptualized and made inferences about human nature, depended as much on the cultural economic substructure as it did on his genius. Freud, Dahmer claims, remained unaware of the economic-cultural overdetermination of his work.

Dahmer's emphasis is on the inseparable intertwining of the fate of individuals and the structure of society at large. The origin of neurosis as well as its cure depends, then, on each person's ability to interact in a constructive manner with the society in which he lives. Man must cope with the illusion society tries to force upon him. While Dahmer states that efforts to implement political Marxism have failed to materialize, he believes that psychoanalysis must combine with a Marxist perspective in order to provide full therapeutic benefits for patients. From this radical viewpoint Dahmer critically reviews "the first generation of Marxian psychoanalysts," as they failed to comprehend psychoanalysis' potential to discern in man's soul the mirror image of the social ills besetting him.

Dahmer—the editor of the German psychoanalytic journal Psyche published by Alexander Mitscherlich—writes with a passion for studying and learning. He knows how to blend his own thoughts with interesting quotations, often from rather divergent sources, into a powerful—although to this reviewer not always convincing—argument presented in what perhaps might best be called a dialectic style. Ideas are often expressed indirectly, so that intermittently the reader must wrestle hidden meanings from the text. The successful effort provides gratification akin to finding a solution to a puzzle, but one doubts whether the complexity of the ideas discussed always necessitates the complexity of the style.

INSIGHTS FROM THE BLIND; COMPARATIVE STUDIES OF BLIND AND SIGHTED INFANTS. By Selma Fraiberg with the collaboration of Louis Fraiberg. New York: Basic Books, 1977, 288 pp.

Professor Selma Fraiberg is one of our foremost investigators of the ego development of blind children. She has designed and demonstrated the best program of intervention for the mental health of blind infants that we know. She is, additionally, one of psychoanalysis' most graceful writers. Her early book, The Magic Years, is widely used in teaching concepts of child development because of its soundness, clarity, and wisdom. It is therefore a special treat to have now Insights from the Blind; Comparative Studies of Blind and Sighted Infants written with the overall collaboration of Louis Fraiberg and with the individual collaboration of co-authors of particular chapters, David Freedman, Barry L. Siegel, Ralph Gibson, and Edna Adelson. This is a summary of her fifteen years of work with blind infants and their parents.

After a summary of observations and a review of the salient literature, she notes that when she began, in 1960, no detailed longitudinal studies of blind babies existed.

Mrs. Fraiberg's first study was of a five-month-old girl, blind from birth, who was visited monthly and recorded developmentally on film. The surprising discovery was made that the smiling response and stranger anxiety took place normally without vision, but interest in toys and mobility were delayed in spite of normal maturation.

Material from the two-and-a-half-year treatment of a nine-year-old autistic blind child is then detailed. The data illuminate problems of the achievement of differentiation of erotic and aggressive drives and the attainment of an object concept.

TOYS AND REASONS: STAGES IN THE RITUALIZATION OF EXPERIENCE. By Erik H. Erikson. New York: W.W. Norton, 1977, 182 pp., $8.95.

This newest volume is based on the Godkin lectures given at Harvard University in 1972, under the title of "Play, Vision and Deception." The subject matter deals with the relationship between childhood play and political imagination, between make-believe and belief, between the legitimate theatre and the theatre of politics and war, between playful vision and serious theory, and, finally, between child's toys and aged reason.

Erikson, an energetic exponent of interdisciplinary conceptualization, weaves back and forth between psychoanalytic insights and political themes, always working from a developmental frame of reference. He bridges his conceptual leaps with an ontogenetic view of ritualization. In the first chapter, he illustrates and justifies the jump from play to vision, with perhaps an overly broad and inclusive use of the term "play." Erikson speaks of a "playful element" (p. 23) in the marches and demonstrations against the Vietnam war, and of the "historical playfulness" (p. 24) of the Columbia University students' confrontation with administration and police, and of the role of "games" and "play" in the vicissitudes of war, business, therapy, and family life. With roots in clinical observation, Erikson begins with the play construction and accompanying story of a five-year-old which is seen as the link between the "builder's inner universe and his society's changing world view" (p. 37). This play is a portent of the child's future and occupies a "recognizable place in that life history" (p. 39).

Play continues to be seen, as it was in Childhood and Society

[[RunningHead]]

The Concept Of "classical" Psychoanalysis

Arthur Valenstein M.D.

Arthur Valenstein

Arthur F. Valenstein, M.D.
140A Foster Street
Cambridge, Mass. 02138

Arthur Valenstein  

THIS PAPER REFLECTS A LONG-STANDING INTEREST in comparative studies of various psychotherapies and different approaches to clinical psychoanalysis. Perhaps it is timely, in this era of the broadly extended scope of psychoanalysis, to consider once again the nature of therapeutic psychoanalysis as such; that is, in the narrower or special sense which is implied when one speaks of "classical psychoanalysis."

When I suggest that reconsideration is indicated at this time I do so advisedly. For example, one of the panels which was on the scientific program of the American Psychoanalytic Association in December 1977 was titled, "The Nature of the Therapeutic Action of Psychoanalysis."1 Is this so different from the main symposium on "The Curative Factors in Psycho-Analysis," held at the 22nd Congress of the International Psycho-Analytical Association in Edinburgh in 1961? Twenty-five years earlier the archetypical scientific meeting on this subject took place, the Marienbad Symposium, in 1936, on "The Theory of the Therapeutic Results of Psycho-Analysis." Are such reappearances of essentially the same topic indicative of a principle of redundancy which, for whatever reason, pervades psychoanalysis and its scientific life?

An earlier version of this paper was presented to the Baltimore-District of Columbia Society for Psychoanalysis on November 19, 1977, and to the Cleveland Psychoanalytic Society on January 20, 1978.113
